explanation: The requirement states that whenever prop_0 holds, both prop_1 and prop_2 should also hold at that moment. In LTL, this is captured by a global implication: globally, if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 and prop_2 are also true at the same time.

The specification states a simple condition: if prop_0, prop_1, and prop_2 are true at any point. There's no further consequence or temporal condition, so the formula is just the conjunction of the three atomic propositions. This would describe a state where all three are true simultaneously.

The statement asserts that 'if prop_0, prop_1 and prop_2'; since no consequent is specified, this is simply a logical conjunction of the propositions, holding wherever the condition is considered. Interpreted as a state in which all three are true together: prop_0 & prop_1 & prop_2. No temporal structure is specified.

The requirement says that whenever 'prop_0' holds, then the system should behave so that both 'prop_1' and 'prop_2' hold. In LTL, this is typically interpreted as: globally, whenever 'prop_0' is true, both 'prop_1' and 'prop_2' must be true at the same time. This is written as 'G (prop_0 -> (prop_1 & prop_2))'.

The property states that whenever prop_0 is true, both prop_1 and prop_2 should also be true at the same time. In LTL, this is expressed as: 'Globally, if prop_0 holds, then both prop_1 and prop_2 must hold.'

This requirement says that whenever prop_0 holds (at any time), then prop_1 and prop_2 must also hold at that same time. In LTL, this is generally expressed as the global (G) property that, whenever prop_0 holds, so do prop_1 and prop_2.

The requirement states that if prop_0 holds, then the system should enforce both prop_1 and prop_2. Translated into LTL, this means that globally, whenever prop_0 is true, both prop_1 and prop_2 are also true at that same point in time. This is captured as: G(prop_0 -> (prop_1 & prop_2))
Whenever prop_0 is true, then both prop_1 and prop_2 should hold at that time. This is most directly represented as: 'Globally, if prop_0 then (prop_1 and prop_2)'.

The requirement is conditional: if prop_0 holds, then the system should ensure prop_1 and prop_2. In LTL, a standard encoding for obligations triggered by conditions is G (p -> q), meaning 'globally, if p then q'. Here, both prop_1 and prop_2 need to hold, so we use their conjunction as the obligation.

Whenever 'prop_0' holds, it is required that both 'prop_1' and 'prop_2' hold at the same moment. This is a standard implication relation, meaning at every time, if 'prop_0' holds, then both must also hold.
